topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and aged bark hit me first, a familiar comfort from my childhood. I stood at the edge of the overgrown garden, tracing the outline of the trunk with a hesitant finger. It was the oak, the one I’d painstakingly nurtured into a young sentinel when I was seven. Now, where the sturdy canopy should have been, there was only a jagged, raw scar on the soil. My breath hitched in my throat. I took a stumbling step closer, my heart beginning a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. The silence of the clearing suddenly felt heavy, oppressive, as if the very air held its breath. I circled the base, my gaze darting from the exposed roots to the empty space where the leaves and branches used to be. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, and I could feel a sudden, sharp pressure behind my eyes. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle in the stillness.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the sheer emptiness to hit me with such force. For years, that sapling had been my tiny responsibility, a symbol of quiet persistence. Now, looking at the stumps—the stark, freshly severed ends of the wood—a wave of profound unease washed over me. My hands started to tremble, the fine hairs on my forearms prickling. I knelt down, pressing my palms flat against the disturbed soil, searching for any sign of struggle, any misplaced tool. Nothing. Just bare earth and the undeniable evidence of what had been there. A sharp intake of breath escaped me, rattling in my chest. I pushed myself up abruptly, my movements jerky. I needed to verify it; I needed to understand how this had happened. My voice, when I finally spoke, came out surprisingly tight, laced with a tremor I couldn't quite mask.

[story 3]

It was the sight that froze me. The space where the young tree had stood was a gaping void, a testament to violent removal. I walked slowly toward the clearing, my footsteps crunching on dry leaves, each step feeling weighted with dread. My eyes scanned the perimeter, searching for any trace of disturbance, any discarded lumber or tell-tale marks. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. A strange hollowness opened up inside my chest, a sensation like a sudden drop in temperature. I stopped directly in front of the devastation. I didn't move for a long moment, just absorbing the finality of the cut. Then, a low, guttural sound escaped my throat—a choked noise of sheer disbelief and distress. I took a hasty step backward, needing distance, needing to process the reality that had just unfolded.
